summary: A brief abstract reviewing the descriptive epidemiology of DSM-IV mental disorders in the US, highlighting their high prevalence, typical onset in childhood or adolescence, and the critical need for early detection and treatment to prevent severe, comorbid adult disorders.

principles.statement: Mental disorders are highly prevalent in the general population.
principles.rationale: Epidemiological data shows that about half of the US population will experience a mental disorder in their lifetime, and a quarter in any given year.
principles.source_quote: These disorders are highly prevalent: Roughly half the population meets criteria for one or more such disorders in their lifetimes, and roughly one fourth of the population meets criteria in any given year.

principles.statement: Public health efforts must focus on the early detection and treatment of child and adolescent mental disorders.
principles.rationale: Most severe, persistent, and highly comorbid adult mental disorders begin as mild, untreated conditions in youth.
principles.source_quote: Given that most seriously impairing and persistent adult mental disorders are associated with child-adolescent onsets and high comorbidity, increased efforts are needed to study the public health implications of early detection and treatment of initially mild and currently largely untreated child-adolescent disorders.

principles.statement: Adult-onset mental disorders are typically comorbid conditions.
principles.rationale: Because primary disorders usually begin in childhood or adolescence, disorders that appear later in life are often secondary or comorbid.
principles.source_quote: Most people with a history of mental disorder had first onsets in childhood or adolescence. Later onsets typically involve comorbid disorders.

signature_quotes.text: Roughly half the population meets criteria for one or more such disorders in their lifetimes, and roughly one fourth of the population meets criteria in any given year.
signature_quotes.context: Describing the high prevalence of DSM-IV mental disorders in the United States.

signature_quotes.text: Most people with a history of mental disorder had first onsets in childhood or adolescence.
signature_quotes.context: Highlighting the early age of onset for most psychiatric conditions.

signature_quotes.text: ...increased efforts are needed to study the public health implications of early detection and treatment of initially mild and currently largely untreated child-adolescent disorders.
signature_quotes.context: Concluding recommendation based on the epidemiological data linking severe adult disorders to untreated childhood onsets.

anti_patterns.description: Leaving mild child and adolescent mental disorders untreated.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: These initially mild conditions often develop into seriously impairing, persistent, and highly comorbid adult mental disorders.
